scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly mentions Eleusis and Athens; Aristophanes is a contemporary observer of Athenian social customs.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Plutus of Aristophanes was produced in 388 BCE, reflecting contemporary Athenian social competition.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The taxation of slaves (andrapoda) and their earnings was a feature of the Athenian fiscal system described by contemporary playwrights.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Plutus was produced in 388 BCE; the mentions of slave taxes reflect contemporary Athenian economic regulations.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The roles of Hydriaphoros and Skiadephoros were mandatory ritual services specifically for metics (resident aliens) in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the production of Aristophanes' Plutus (388 BCE), observing the contemporary status of metics.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Thesmophoria was a major Athenian festival where the exclusion of slaves was a strictly enforced civic-religious rule.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play Plutus (388 BCE) reflects the contemporary Athenian religious prohibitions regarding slaves.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law famously exempted emporoi (wholesale merchants) from military service to protect the grain supply.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristophanes' Plutus (388 BCE) alludes to these contemporary exemptions as part of the Athenian legal landscape.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Public fees (misthos) for heliasts and assembly-goers were a cornerstone of Athenian democratic citizenship.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the established democratic practices in Athens at the time of the production of Plutus in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aeschines is an Athenian orator citing specific Athenian statutory laws (nomoi) governing the liturgy of the choregia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines' speech Against Timarchus was delivered in 345 BCE, citing laws in force at that time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The orator cites a specific provision of Athenian law regarding 'scurrilous conduct' and the maintenance of parents.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the delivery of Against Timarchus in 345 BCE, describing the contemporary legal code of Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The law of hubris in Athens uniquely allowed for the protection of slaves from physical outrage, as cited by Aeschines.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines refers to this as a standing law in his speech delivered in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law dictated that citizens who prostituted themselves suffered atimia (loss of civic rights), including eligibility for the archonship.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines quotes this law as active during the prosecution of Timarchus in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion from the Bema (speaking platform) was a primary legal consequence of atimia in the Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated as a contemporary legal prohibition in Aeschines' speech of 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Eligibility for embassies and heraldship was restricted to citizens in good standing under Athenian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites these specific civic disabilities as part of the contemporary legal code in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Procedural precedence for elders in the Ekklesia was a traditional rule of order in the Athenian assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines describes the contemporary heraldic formula used in the assembly in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'dokimasia rhetoron' (scrutiny of orators) in Athens disqualified those who mistreated their parents (kakosis goneon).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Cited as a core legal requirement for public speakers in Aeschines' 345 BCE speech.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law linked political speech rights to the fulfillment of military obligations (strateia) and cowardice laws.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary Athenian legal standard for citizen eligibility in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The prohibition on speakers who had engaged in prostitution was the central legal argument in Aeschines' case.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This specific law was the basis of the trial of Timarchus in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law disqualified citizens who wasted their inheritance from addressing the assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines cites this as part of the 'scrutiny of orators' in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The procedure of 'epangelia' allowed citizens to challenge the standing of speakers in the Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines describes the mechanism of the suit of scrutiny as it functioned in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Radical Athenian democracy explicitly permitted the participation of thematic workers (banausoi) and those without noble birth.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines contrasts the general openness of the assembly with specific moral disqualifications in 345 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law imposed a set fine (lipomartyriou) for citizens who failed to testify when summoned.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aeschines details the financial penalty for refusing a summons in his 345 BCE litigation.
